one_line_takeaway: PatchIsland reframes LLM-based automated vulnerability repair around the operational realities of continuous fuzzing (duplicate crashes, agent/provider failures, multi-day autonomous operation) via an agent ensemble, patch-based two-phase deduplication, and FP2 scheduling, and empirically outperformed two other real AIxCC competition systems (84/92 vs 57/92 vs 16/92 on the internal benchmark) while achieving the highest patch count (31) in the actual AIxCC final.
key_finding: On the 92-vulnerability internal benchmark, PatchIsland repaired 84/92 (91.3%), versus Buttercup at 57/92 (62.0%) and RoboDuck at 16/92 (17.4%), with PatchIsland showing 0% error rate versus RoboDuck's 21.7% and Buttercup's 8.7% error rate. The ensemble best-of-5-agents reached 87/92 (94.6%); individual agents ranged from Vincent (87/92, 94.6% -- note: table shows Vincent's row totals as 87/92 alongside ClaudeLike 76/92, Martian 84/92, MultiRetrieval 84/92, Prism 83/92). FP2 orchestration let the top-priority agent alone resolve 91% of eventual successes, giving a time/cost balance between pure Sequential and pure Parallel strategies. In the actual AIxCC final, PatchIsland produced the most patches of any competing team (31, from 43 organizer-confirmed vulnerabilities out of 53 CPs) and the second-highest success rate (72.1%); restricting to intended vulnerabilities the paper's own reconstructed success rate was 27/33 = 82.8%. PatchIsland also independently generated a patch for a real 0-day in PDFBox that the paper reports as identical to the fix later adopted by the project maintainer. Build-time optimizations reported: ccache applicable to 107/110 (97.3%) buildable C projects with a 55% time-weighted average build-time reduction; Maven dependency sharing gave a 66% build-time reduction for Java projects.
core_contribution: PatchIsland is a Continuous Vulnerability Repair (CVR) system that integrates LLM-based Automated Vulnerability Repair (AVR) with continuous fuzzing pipelines (e.g., OSS-Fuzz), rather than the static, single-run benchmark setting used by prior AVR work. Its own novel contributions are: (1) an 'ensemble of agents' ('buckets of agents') strategy where five in-house agents (MultiRetrieval, Vincent, Martian, ClaudeLike, Prism) plus optionally integrated external agents (Codex, Claude Code, SWE-Agent, Aider) independently attempt patches to broaden search coverage and isolate per-agent failures; (2) a two-phase patch-based deduplication algorithm (crash-side dedup on arrival against existing patches, and patch-side dedup/merging when new patches are generated) to handle duplicate crashes/patches typical of continuous fuzzing, given that stack-based or fuzzer-specific dedup heuristics (cited as prior work) are described as inaccurate or engine-specific; (3) FP2 orchestration (First-come-first-served result selection, Preference-based sequential agent ordering, Provider-aware parallel scheduling to avoid LLM API throttling) as a middle ground between pure sequential and pure parallel agent execution; and (4) Crete, an internal framework providing shared infrastructure (OSS-Fuzz environment management, patch evaluator, fault localizer, code retriever, environment pooling/caching) to reduce agent-development effort. The system was built by a KAIST/POSTECH/Georgia Tech/Korea University/Samsung/Microsoft team and deployed as a real Cyber Reasoning System (CRS) in DARPA's AIxCC competition.
threat_model: The paper does not define an adversarial threat model in the traditional sense (it does not model an attacker). The 'defender' setting is: real-world open-source C/Java codebases (e.g., curl, OpenSSL, log4j, PDFBox, Wireshark) contain vulnerabilities discovered by continuous fuzzing (OSS-Fuzz-style crash reports / Proofs-of-Vulnerability). PatchIsland's job is to autonomously generate correct, semantics-preserving patches for these crashes without human intervention, under DARPA AIxCC competition constraints (finite compute/LLM budget, no human interaction after deployment, must operate continuously over multi-day competition windows, patches manually validated post-hoc by organizers rather than trusted on plausibility alone).
limitations: Limitations stated or evidenced in the paper itself: (1) Plausibility-vs-correctness gap -- PatchIsland cannot automatically verify patch correctness, only plausibility (passes PoV suppression + functional tests); the postmortem found 1/33 plausible-but-incorrect patch for intended vulnerabilities versus 10/14 for unintended vulnerabilities (which lack well-designed functional tests), including a concrete case where a patch merely wrapped a JSON deserialization call in a top-level exception handler instead of fixing the root cause. (2) 'Superman patch' risk -- the patch-merging step in two-phase dedup can produce a single overly broad patch addressing multiple distinct vulnerabilities at once (demonstrated in a shadowsocks-libev case fixing 3 intended bugs with one patch), which the authors flag as complicating the notion of patch equivalence for AVR benchmarking. (3) A single point of failure was found in production: a broken symlink in the systemd challenge project (etc/os-release -> ../usr/lib/os-release) broke PatchIsland's timestamp-normalization logic in the Coordinator, causing total failure to patch that project despite the ensemble design -- 0/3 intended systemd vulnerabilities patched during the final versus 4/4 in the offline benchmark. (4) Baseline comparison threat to validity acknowledged by the authors: RoboDuck and Buttercup were isolated to only their patch-generation components (stripped from their full CRS workflow), which the authors admit may have altered implicit dependencies/assumptions in the baselines' original behavior. (5) Incomplete postmortem data -- system logs and artifacts were partially lost due to volume constraints, and ground-truth vulnerability/CP mappings had to be manually reconstructed by the authors after the competition rather than obtained from organizers. (6) FP2's 'operational robustness' benefit is argued qualitatively/by reasoning, not measured with a quantitative robustness metric, as the authors themselves state.

datasets_used: 92 synthetic-vulnerability PoV benchmark drawn from AIxCC final-round Challenge Projects across 24 real-world OSS C/Java projects (e.g., Apache Commons Compress, Apache POI, curl, FreeRDP, libxml2, PDFBox, Wireshark, XZ, systemd, log4j2, shadowsocks-libev, dav1d, lcms, libavif, libexif, mongoose, Tika)
AIxCC final competition itself: 53 Challenge Projects derived from 28 repositories (~54M LOC), 70 intended vulnerabilities, evaluated 6 hours delta mode / 12 hours full mode per CP
quantitative_results: Internal benchmark: PatchIsland 84/92 (91.3%) vs Buttercup 57/92 (62.0%) vs RoboDuck 16/92 (17.4%)
Per-agent totals (5 runs each, 92 PoVs): ClaudeLike 76/92 (82.6%), Martian 84/92 (91.3%), MultiRetrieval 84/92 (91.3%), Prism 83/92 (90.2%), Vincent 87/92 (94.6%)
Top-priority agent under FP2 orchestration resolved 91% of eventual successful cases

AIxCC final: PatchIsland submitted 42 patches; organizers confirmed 31 correct out of 43 total confirmed vulnerabilities -- reported repair rate 72.1%, highest patch count and second-highest success rate among all competing teams
Reconstructed intended-vulnerability success rate: 27/33 correct patches = 82.8%
Unintended vulnerabilities: 14 patches generated, 4 correct / 10 plausible-but-incorrect; one correct patch fixed a genuine 0-day in PDFBox
Deduplication: 42 patches deduplicated 65 of 122 total PoVs (53.3%); 17 PoVs from 2 undeduplicated vulnerabilities where patch generation failed
ccache applicable to 107/110 (97.3%) buildable OSS-Fuzz C projects, 55% time-weighted average build-time reduction; Maven dependency sharing gave 66% Java build-time reduction
Agent-integration effort (lines of code): Codex 143, Claude Code 390, SWE-Agent 570, Aider 646
Total team compute/LLM spend across all AIxCC final competitors: $359K
baselines_compared: RoboDuck (AIxCC competition system, isolated patch-generation component only)
Buttercup (AIxCC competition system, isolated patch-generation component only)
